article_title: Police License Plate Networks Used for School Enrollment Checks, Background Investigations, and Noise Complaints, EFF Finds
summary: All facts verified across sources; allowlist warnings are policy notes only — no factual issues found

content_preview.title: Police License Plate Networks Used for School Enrollment Checks, Background Investigations, and Noise Complaints, EFF Finds
content_preview.summary: An EFF analysis of millions of Flock Safety ALPR searches reveals law enforcement using crime-fighting surveillance tools for school residency fraud checks, employment background investigations, and noise complaints — with no warrant required.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

A new investigation by the Electronic Frontier Foundation has documented a pattern of law enforcement agencies using Flock Safety's automated license plate reader network — a system marketed as a crime-fighting tool — to conduct warrantless surveillance for purposes ranging from school enrollment disputes to noise complaints and employment background checks. According to [the EFF report](https://www.eff.org/deeplinks/2026/05/more-license-plate-reader-mission-creep-school-residency-v...

recommendations: Consider adding nbcchicago.com, opb.org, and cascadiadaily.com to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Thorough investigative article (1260 words, top of News category range) based on an EFF investigation. Well-structured with specific data points, named agencies, individual case study, and legislative context.
editor_notes.source_verification: source-0.html.gz (EFF report): confirmed all key Buford statistics (375+ searches, 5,800+ networks, three-quarters in Q1 2026), employment search details for all 5 agencies with exact counts and network spans, 26 agencies for noise complaints, EFF quotes ('universal tracker', 'every time a plate is searched'). source-1.html.gz (NBC Chicago): confirmed Thalía Sánchez case details, $41,904 contract, 36-month term, Thomson Reuters Clear description, Sánchez quote verbatim, District 126 website statement. source-2.html.gz (The Register): confirmed 'all the required documents' description. source-3.html.gz (EFF March 2026): confirmed Georgia State Patrol traffic enforcement incident and Flock's November 2025 blog post disclaimer. source-4.html.gz (OPB): confirmed Oregon SB 1516, signed March 31 by Gov. Tina Kotek, 30-day retention limit, public audits, civil suit provision, Ky Fireside 'skirt that aspect' quote. source-5.html.gz (Cascadia Daily): confirmed Washington law signed March 30, felony/gross misdemeanor limitation, health care/worship bans, immigration enforcement prohibition, out-of-state sharing ban, nearly 100 law enforcement + 100 private businesses, ACLU 'Before this law, there were no regulations' quote, Flock 'We are very happy to be regulated' quote.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All key statistics, named agencies, quotes, and legislative details verified verbatim from sources. The article correctly attributes each fact to its source. No fabrications detected.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral presentation of investigative findings. The article appropriately reports advocacy organization's views without endorsing them editorially.
editor_notes.originality: No prior ALPR/Flock Safety articles in recent archive. Novel investigative coverage.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Thoroughly sourced investigative journalism article with all claims verified. APPROVE.
